introHeading: Are we onto something — or just renaming it?
introLede:
Honesty is the whole point of this project, so here's the uncomfortable version: storytree
        didn't invent any of its parts. The interesting question is whether putting them together is a
        real advance or a tidy rename. We went and surveyed the field to find out — and we'll show you
        the case against, not just the case for.
introBody1: The binding constraint on an AI coding agent isn't raw intelligence. It's the context window and the lack of memory between sessions — an agent works inside a finite attention budget, and when the session ends it forgets. The instinct is to give it more: a bigger window, the whole repo pasted in. The most thoughtful practitioners say that's backwards. The better move is to manage attention, not expand it — give the agent a map it reads on demand and writes back to, so state lives outside the prompt and survives the session.
introBody2: storytree splits that map into three surfaces: a library (what's known — decisions, definitions, guidance), a code map (what the system is — the codebase as an authored DAG), and a work-board (what's being done — a dependency-aware task tier). The survey below tests that model against what everyone else has actually shipped, scored from primary sources.
surveyHeading: Everyone's built a piece. We looked for who'd built all three.
surveyBody:
Each cohort builds one axis and stops. The code-map cohort — Aider's repo map, CodeGraph,
        Augment, Cody — gives an agent a view of the code and nothing else. The library cohort —
        Anthropic's Skills and memory, Letta's memory blocks — builds the knowledge tier and no code map.
        Steve Yegge's Beads and Gas Town land squarely on the work-board. Geoffrey Huntley's loom has
        the raw components — thread search, a docs publisher, VCS tooling — but none is presented as an
        integrated, agent-facing surface. Scored against the three surfaces, it looks like this:
surveyLegendNote: A caution we take seriously: this is the surveyed sources at one date. Several named players — Cursor, Cline, Devin, Factory, OpenHands, Windsurf — produced no verified claims in this pass and are deliberately left off the board. Absence of evidence isn't evidence of absence; any of them could be building the triad unobserved here.
newHeading: Two things we didn't find anyone else doing.
newBody1: One: the unification. No tool we surveyed builds all three surfaces as one coherent model. That's it — not a capability nobody else could build, just a combination we didn't find anyone holding. When independent teams keep arriving at the same primitives, the primitives are probably right; storytree's bet is that you arrive faster if you draw the whole map at once instead of one wall at a time.
newBody2: Two: the altitude. The code-map tools operate at symbol altitude — a bottom-up mirror of the code as written, extracted mechanically from source. The map reflects the code; change the code and it re-mirrors. storytree's code map is the other way around: it's authored top-down as intent, and the code has to satisfy it. A story is the size of a service; a capability is an organ inside it. Spec-driven tools like Spec Kit and Kiro do author intent top-down too — they're the closest neighbours here — but they tend to flatten it into per-feature checklists that are thrown away when the feature ships, rather than a standing, architectural map that outlives any one task.
newCaveat:
Both claims are narrow on purpose. “Distinctive” here means “not found in this survey” — not
        “proven novel.” We'd rather under-claim and be right.
convergedHeading: Smart people walked toward these surfaces from different directions.
convergedBody:
The strongest evidence that the individual pieces are sound is that the field built them
        independently, piece by piece, without coordinating. Convergent evolution is a useful signal.
        Here's the dated version — every entry traces to a primary source, and the soft dates are flagged.
honestHeading: The strongest argument that this is nothing.
honestLede: Task trackers, docs-and-memory tiers, and VCS-backed code views already exist — in unintegrated form. Maybe storytree just labels and arranges primitives that Beads, Skills, and Aider already shipped, and the “unification” is a presentation choice, not a capability. On that reading, the contribution is mostly naming.
honestBody:
The honest counter is narrow: the three surfaces are a pattern the field independently
            converged on; no surveyed tool unifies all three; and the authored, architecture-altitude
            code map is a genuinely different object from the bottom-up mirrors everyone else built.
            Whether that adds up to a real advance or a tidy rename is the open question — and it's more
            useful to leave it open than to answer it louder than the evidence allows.

caveats.text: It's a point-in-time snapshot. The sources span roughly 2023–mid 2026 and the fast movers move fast. “No one unifies all three” is a finding about these sources at this date, not a guarantee about the field.

The mappings are our framing. Casting Beads as a work-board, Skills as a library, or Aider's repo map as a code map is our interpretation. None of these projects describes itself in storytree's “three surfaces” language.
There are coverage gaps. Several known tools produced no verified claims this pass and are off the board. Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence.

Some internals are genuinely contested. The embedding-vs-graph reading of Augment and Cody was challenged during verification, so the code-map cohort shouldn't be read as a uniform family of structural graphs — we mark the surface, not the mechanism.
“Distinctive” ≠ “first.” Where we call the unification or the altitude distinctive, that's a statement about what this survey did and didn't surface — not a claim of first-ever invention.

honestSrc:
The marks above are scored from primary sources, linked inline throughout this page and
            consolidated from three adversarially-verified research passes (Jun 2026). Where a claim
            was refuted in verification — including two “agent momentum” citations — we dropped it rather
            than dress it up.
ctaLede:
If the bet makes sense to you — or if you think it's a rename and want to say so — that's exactly
        the kind of conversation this is built for.
ctaPrimary: See how the three fit together →
